segments.text: Lesson 3
こんにちは!
Some of you may be wondering how we got through two lessons without mentioning the は particle.
Most courses introduce は from the beginning with sentences like:

“わたし は あめりかじん だ”
“ぺん は あおい”
But starting this way can make Japanese structure confusing because は does not work like が を or に.
The important point is this:
は can never be part of the core sentence.
It is not the main carriage A and it is not the engine B. It is also not a white carriage like を because を is part of the logical structure of the sentence.
A noun marked with は is not part of the logical structure. は is a non-logical particle. So what does は do?
It marks the topic.
That is why we can picture は as a flag. It simply flags what the sentence is about. It does not tell us what is logically happening in the sentence. For example:
“わたし は あめりかじん だ”
This literally means: “As for me I am an American.”

“わたし は” means “as for me”.
“あめりかじん だ” means “am American”.
But something is missing from the logical sentence: the invisible が-car. The full structure is:
“わたし は zero が あめりかじん だ”
So the meaning is: “As for me I am an American.”
It may be tempting to pretend that “わたし は” is the subject of the sentence but that only works sometimes. In other cases it gives the wrong meaning. A famous example is:
“わたし は うなぎ だ”
うなぎ means eel.
If we wrongly treat は as the subject marker we might think this means:
“I am an eel.”
But that is not what it means in context. The full structure is:
“わたし は zero が うなぎ だ”
Here zero does not mean “I”. It means “it” — the thing being discussed such as what someone is ordering for dinner. So the real meaning is:
“As for me it is eel.”
Or more naturally: “I’ll have the eel.”
This shows why は must not be treated as the subject marker. It only marks the topic. The logical subject is still marked by が even when it is invisible.
Now let’s introduce another white carriage: に.
に forms a useful trio with が and を. In an “A does B” sentence:

が tells us who does the action.
を tells us what the action is done to.
に tells us the target or destination of the action.
For example:
“わたし が ぼーる を なげる”
ぼーる means ball and なげる means throw.
So this means: “I throw a ball.”
The core sentence is:
“わたし が なげる” — “I throw.”
The を carriage tells us what I throw: “ぼーる を” — “a ball.”
Now we can add に:
“わたし が ぼーる を さくら に なげる”

This means:
“I throw a ball to Sakura”
or
“I throw a ball at Sakura.”
さくら に marks Sakura as the target or destination of the throwing.
The key point is that Japanese particles tell us the logic of the sentence. Word order is much less important than in English. For example:
“わたし に さくら が ぼーる を なげる”

This means:
“Sakura throws the ball at me.”

And:
“ぼーる が わたし に さくら を なげる”

This means:
“The ball throws Sakura at me.”

That sentence is strange but grammatically the particles still tell us who is doing what to whom.
Now let’s bring は back into the sentence.
“わたし は さくら に ぼーる を なげる”

The full structure is:
“わたし は zero が さくら に ぼーる を なげる”

This means:
“As for me I throw the ball at Sakura.”

Now let’s move は to the ball:
“ぼーる は わたし が さくら に なげる”

This means:
“As for the ball I throw it at Sakura.”

Notice the difference.
When we change logical particles like が を or に the actual logic of the sentence changes.
But when we move は the logic does not change. Only the topic or emphasis changes.So:

“わたし は...” means we are talking about me.
“ぼーる は...” means we are talking about the ball.
But who throws what is thrown and who it is thrown to are still decided by が を and に.
That is the difference between logical particles and the non-logical topic particle は.
